🚨 MARKET UPDATE LATEST — TUESDAY, 14 JULY 2026

US inflation cooled far more sharply than expected in June.

Headline prices recorded their largest monthly decline since April 2020, while underlying inflation was completely flat.

At the same time, America’s biggest banks reported blockbuster profits—showing that consumers, credit markets and Wall Street remain remarkably resilient.

This is the combination markets wanted:

cooler inflation without an economic collapse.

But July’s renewed oil shock means the Fed still cannot declare victory.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 US INFLATION DELIVERS A MAJOR DOWNSIDE SURPRISE

June CPI was cooler than expected across every major measure:

🔸 Headline CPI MoM: −0.4%
Forecast: approximately −0.1%
Previous: +0.5%

🔸 Headline CPI YoY: 3.5%
Forecast: approximately 3.8%–3.9%
Previous: 4.2%

🔸 Core CPI MoM: 0.0%
Forecast: approximately +0.2%–0.3%
Previous: +0.2%

🔸 Core CPI YoY: 2.6%
Forecast: approximately 2.8%–2.9%
Previous: 2.9%

The 0.4% fall in overall consumer prices was the largest monthly decline since April 2020.

The most encouraging number, however, was core CPI.

Prices excluding food and energy did not rise at all during June.
That suggests the improvement was not limited entirely to cheaper petrol.

Simple explanation

Headline CPI measures the entire shopping basket.
Core CPI removes volatile food and energy prices to reveal the underlying trend.
Both cooled sharply—and the core basket was completely unchanged during June.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 WHY INFLATION FELL MORE THAN EXPECTED

The details of the report were even more encouraging than the headline numbers.

🔸 Energy prices fell 5.7% in June, their biggest monthly decline since April 2020.

🔸 Petrol prices dropped 9.7%, driving much of the overall fall in inflation.

🔸 Shelter inflation rose just 0.1%, its smallest monthly increase since January 2021, suggesting housing costs are finally easing.

Lower fuel prices reduced transport and energy costs, while slower rent increases removed one of the biggest sources of persistent inflation.

Together, they produced a much softer report than markets expected.

However, June captured the period when the temporary US–Iran de-escalation pushed oil prices lower.

That relief may not last.

The ceasefire has since deteriorated, fighting has resumed, shipping through the Strait of Hormuz is again under pressure, and oil has risen sharply in July.

Simple explanation

Today’s CPI report is like looking in the rear-view mirror.

It tells us that petrol became cheaper and housing inflation slowed during June.

It does not yet show the effect of the renewed oil rise happening now.

That is why one excellent inflation report does not guarantee that July and August will be equally benign.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THE PRESSURE FOR AN IMMEDIATE FED HIKE HAS EASED

Before the report, futures markets were pricing roughly a 41% probability of a Federal Reserve rate increase at its next meeting.

Today’s softer figures weaken the argument for an immediate hike.

The combination of:

🔸 falling headline prices;

🔸 flat monthly core inflation;

🔸 and sharply lower annual readings;

gives the Fed more room to wait and assess whether the renewed oil shock spreads into the wider economy.

But inflation remains above target.

Headline CPI is still 3.5%.

Core CPI is still 2.6%.

And the Fed’s preferred PCE measure has recently been running considerably hotter.

Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh’s testimony before Congress therefore becomes even more important. 

Markets will watch whether he treats June’s report as evidence that inflation is genuinely returning towards target—or merely temporary relief before July’s oil increase appears in the data.

Simple explanation

The Fed now has less reason to raise rates immediately.

But it still needs to see several months of improvement before becoming confident that inflation is under control.

One good reading creates breathing space.

It does not finish the job.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 WALL STREET BANKS DELIVER BLOCKBUSTER PROFITS

The inflation surprise arrived alongside exceptional results from five of America’s largest banks.

Every one reported higher second-quarter profits.

💠 JPMorgan Chase

🔸 Net income: $21.2 billion, up 41%

🔸 Profit excluding the Visa-related gain: approximately $16.9 billion

🔸 Equity-trading revenue: a record $6 billion

🔸 Total trading revenue: $12.1 billion, up 35%

🔸 2026 net-interest-income forecast raised to $105.5 billion

JPMorgan’s equity traders generated approximately 50% more revenue than analysts expected.

The bank also reduced its forecast for credit-card losses—an encouraging indication that household credit remains relatively healthy.

💠 Goldman Sachs

🔸 Profit: $6.6 billion, up almost 80%

🔸 Equity-trading revenue: a record $7.4 billion, up 72%

🔸 Investment-banking fees: $3.4 billion, up more than 50%

Goldman recorded its strongest quarterly profit in five years, driven by record equity trading and stronger underwriting and dealmaking activity.

💠 Bank of America

🔸 Profit: $9.1 billion, up 27%

🔸 Revenue: $31.6 billion, up 15%

🔸 Equity-trading revenue: a record $3.6 billion, up 70%

🔸 Net-interest income: $16.2 billion, up 9%

Despite beating expectations, Bank of America shares initially fell in pre-market trading.

That appears to be a classic case of investors selling the news after expectations had already risen.

💠 Citigroup

🔸 Profit: $5.8 billion, up 45%

🔸 Revenue: $24.8 billion, its highest quarterly level in a decade

🔸 Markets revenue: $7 billion

🔸 Investment-banking revenue: $1.9 billion

💠 Wells Fargo

🔸 Profit: $6.4 billion, up 17%

🔸 Revenue: $22.6 billion

Wells Fargo exceeded forecasts as it continued expanding after regulators removed the asset cap that had constrained its balance sheet.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 WHY THE BANKS PERFORMED SO WELL

The standout engine was equity trading.

Banks benefited from:

🔸 major volatility in AI and semiconductor shares;

🔸 the SpaceX flotation;

🔸 the SK Hynix US listing;

🔸 stronger stock issuance;

🔸 large index rebalancing;

🔸 and rising client trading activity.

Simple explanation

Banks earn more trading revenue when investors frequently buy, sell, hedge and raise capital.

Volatility may be uncomfortable for ordinary investors.

But for large Wall Street trading desks, it can create enormous business.

The results also suggest that the underlying economy remains resilient.

Bank executives reported:

🔸 continued consumer spending;

🔸 healthy loan demand;

🔸 manageable credit losses;

🔸 and strong corporate activity.

Jamie Dimon said the global economy had been more resilient to the oil shock than JPMorgan expected.

However, he also warned that geopolitical conflict, sticky inflation, large fiscal deficits and elevated asset prices were shifting beneath the surface “like tectonic plates”.

That is the right balance.

The economy remains strong.

The risks have not disappeared.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔴 IBM DELIVERS THE DAY’S CORPORATE SHOCK

Away from the banks, IBM unexpectedly released preliminary second-quarter results ahead of schedule.

The company reported:

🔸 Revenue: $17.2 billion, below expectations

🔸 Adjusted EPS: $2.93, versus approximately $3.01 expected

🔸 Software revenue growth: 5%

🔸 Infrastructure revenue: down 7%

IBM said customers redirected spending towards memory, storage and servers ahead of expected price increases.

That weakened demand for its mainframe systems and associated software products.

IBM shares fell approximately 23% in pre-market trading, placing the stock on course for one of its worst sessions in decades.

This is an important warning for technology investors.

The AI infrastructure boom is creating enormous winners.

But it is also forcing customers to choose where they allocate limited capital.

Money moving towards chips, memory and data-centre capacity does not benefit every technology company equally.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 WHAT IT MEANS

Today’s developments send six important messages.

1️⃣ The inflation report was genuinely positive

Every major CPI measure cooled.

The flat monthly core reading was particularly encouraging.

2️⃣ An immediate Fed hike is less likely

The Fed now has stronger justification to wait rather than respond aggressively to July’s oil increase.

3️⃣ June and July are telling different stories

June benefited from falling petrol prices.

July has brought renewed fighting, shipping disruption and higher oil.

4️⃣ Inflation cooled without the economy collapsing

Strong bank results and resilient consumer credit suggest the US is not entering an immediate recession.

5️⃣ Market volatility is generating enormous profits

Trading-heavy banks are thriving as clients respond to AI volatility, IPOs and shifting asset prices.

6️⃣ The AI boom is producing clear winners and losers

Semiconductor, memory and infrastructure spending remains strong.

IBM demonstrates that capital can be redirected away from other technology categories very quickly.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 BOTTOM LINE

Today delivered the combination investors wanted:

🔸cooler inflation and stronger corporate profits.

🔸June CPI reduced the immediate pressure on the Federal Reserve.

🔸Bank earnings showed that households, businesses and capital markets remain healthier than feared.

That is supportive for risk assets.

But investors should not confuse one encouraging report with the end of the inflation problem.

🔸June’s CPI reflected falling petrol prices that have since reversed.

🔸The struggle over the Strait of Hormuz remains unresolved.

🔸Oil continues to connect geopolitics with inflation, bond yields and interest-rate policy.

For now, the message is encouraging:

Inflation cooled without the economy breaking.

The next test is whether that progress survives July’s renewed energy shock.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💭 THOUGHT FOR THE DAY

“Good investors react to the number. Great investors understand what created the number—and whether it can last.”

One report can change market sentiment.

Only a sustained trend can change the market cycle.

Sources: US Bureau of Labor Statistics | The Wall Street Journal | Financial Times | IBM | JPMorgan Chase | Goldman Sachs | Bank of America | Citigroup | Wells Fargo | MarketWatch | Barron’s

☀️ Premarket Briefing | Tuesday, July 14, 2026
Earnings, Inflation, and Capitol Hill Take Center Stage
Hello traders and investors,

Today's session has the potential to set the tone for the rest of the week.

After a difficult start to the week that saw semiconductor stocks tumble and oil prices surge on escalating geopolitical tensions, investors now have a full slate of market-moving catalysts to digest. Major bank earnings are arriving before the opening bell, the June Consumer Price Index will provide the latest inflation update, and Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh is scheduled to testify before Congress later today.

At the same time, semiconductor stocks are attempting to recover some of Monday's losses, while higher oil prices continue creating uncertainty as developments surrounding Iran remain in focus.

Let's get you ready for the opening bell.

📊 Futures Mixed Ahead of a Busy Session

As of this morning:

S&P 500 Futures: -19

Nasdaq Futures: +129

Futures are sending mixed signals as investors balance improving sentiment in technology against continued strength in crude oil.

The Nasdaq is positioned to outperform before the opening bell as semiconductor stocks recover part of yesterday's decline. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average remain under pressure, weighed down in part by one of today's biggest corporate stories involving IBM.

With earnings, inflation data, and Federal Reserve commentary all scheduled for today, traders are preparing for one of the busiest sessions of the month.

💻 Semiconductor Stocks Attempt to Stabilize

Technology is showing early signs of improvement.

Semiconductor shares are rebounding after Monday's broad-based selloff, helping lift Nasdaq futures despite continued geopolitical uncertainty.

The recovery comes after several volatile sessions in which chipmakers have experienced sharp swings in both directions. Whether today's buying continues after the opening bell could play an important role in determining whether technology regains market leadership.

One additional development attracting attention comes from NVIDIA, which the Financial Times reports is reducing authorized artificial intelligence customers in Asia by 50% as part of an effort to limit products being diverted into China.

🛢 Oil Remains Elevated

Energy markets remain firmly in focus.

Oil prices continue climbing after President Trump officially notified Congress of a new war with Iran, according to Politico.

Higher energy prices have become one of the market's primary concerns over the past week as investors evaluate the potential impact on inflation, consumer spending, and corporate profit margins.

While technology is attempting to recover this morning, elevated crude prices continue acting as an important counterweight for the broader market.

🏦 Earnings Season Begins in Earnest

Today marks the unofficial beginning of second-quarter earnings season.

Several major banking companies have already reported results before the opening bell, with most delivering results that comfortably exceeded analyst expectations.

Strong reports from the financial sector provide investors with an early look at corporate performance heading into what is expected to become a very active earnings calendar over the coming weeks.

📊 Inflation Report Takes Center Stage

The market's biggest scheduled event arrives this morning.

The June Consumer Price Index (CPI) will be released before the opening bell.

Economists expect June CPI to decline 0.1%.

The report takes on added importance after Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller commented yesterday that elevated inflation could justify a near-term interest rate hike.

Investors will be looking closely for evidence that inflation pressures tied to higher oil prices are beginning to ease.

🏛 Fed Chair Kevin Warsh Heads to Capitol Hill

Investors will also hear directly from Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh later today.

Warsh is scheduled to testify before Congress, giving markets another opportunity to evaluate the Federal Reserve's outlook on inflation, interest rates, and economic conditions.

His comments could become increasingly important depending on how the market reacts to this morning's CPI report.

📰 Corporate Headlines

🏦 Goldman Sachs Delivers Strong Results
Goldman Sachs exceeded earnings expectations by $6.47 per share while also reporting revenue above analyst estimates.

The strong report adds to the positive tone surrounding this morning's bank earnings.

🏦 JPMorgan Chase Also Beats Expectations

JPMorgan Chase reported earnings that exceeded expectations by $2.11 per share and also topped revenue estimates.

The results reinforce the generally solid start to earnings season among major financial institutions.

💻 IBM Drops After Weak Guidance

IBM is one of the market's biggest premarket movers after issuing second-quarter guidance below analyst expectations.

The company projected earnings of $2.93 per share on $17.2 billion in revenue, both below FactSet consensus estimates.

The disappointing outlook is weighing heavily on Dow futures before the opening bell.

🤖 NVIDIA Adjusts AI Customer Strategy

The Financial Times reports that NVIDIA is reducing its authorized artificial intelligence customers across Asia by 50% as part of efforts to limit product diversion into China.

The move comes amid continued regulatory scrutiny surrounding advanced AI chip exports.

🌏 Overseas Markets

Asian markets delivered a mostly positive session overnight.

China led the region with a 1.4% gain.

Japan and South Korea each advanced 0.7%, while Hong Kong added 0.5%.

India was the only major market to finish lower.

The improvement across much of Asia comes despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainty and continued volatility in the semiconductor industry.

💭 My Take

Today's market has no shortage of catalysts.

It's rare to see a major inflation report, Federal Reserve testimony, and the unofficial start of earnings season all arrive on the same day. Any one of those events can influence market direction. Together, they have the potential to shape expectations for the remainder of the week.

I'll be paying particularly close attention to the market's reaction rather than simply the headlines.

Semiconductors are attempting to stabilize after Monday's selling, while financials have delivered a solid start to earnings season. At the same time, higher oil prices continue creating uncertainty as geopolitical tensions remain elevated.

If inflation comes in better than expected and investors respond positively to this morning's bank earnings, technology could have an opportunity to regain leadership. If inflation surprises to the upside or oil continues climbing, yesterday's defensive tone could quickly return.

Today's session should provide much clearer direction heading into the heart of earnings season.

📊 Stocks in Focus

GS: Goldman Sachs beat earnings expectations by $6.47 per share and exceeded revenue estimates.

JPM: JPMorgan Chase topped both earnings and revenue expectations to begin second-quarter earnings season.

IBM: Shares are sharply lower in premarket trading after issuing second-quarter earnings and revenue guidance below analyst expectations.

NVDA: The Financial Times reports NVIDIA is reducing authorized artificial intelligence customers across Asia by 50% as part of efforts to prevent products from being diverted into China.

📝 Stay Connected

Stay tuned for further updates throughout today's trading session on Substack Notes, where I'll be covering the market's reaction to the June CPI report, major bank earnings, Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh's congressional testimony, and any developing market-moving headlines as they happen. Be sure to enable notifications so you don't miss any important updates throughout the day.

Just in: U.S. stocks before the market opens: Futures on the three major stock indexes have mixed gains and losses. IBM plunges more than 20% before the market opens. The market awaits Wash’s testimony and June CPI.

Before the market opened on Tuesday, the three major U.S. stock index futures were mixed, while major European indexes generally fell. As of press time, Nasdaq 100 futures were up 0.52%, Dow futures were down 0.59%, and S&P 500 futures were down 0.09%.

In terms of commodities, Brent crude oil rose 3.76% to US$86.43/barrel; WTI crude oil rose 2.05% to US$79.74/barrel. Spot gold rose 0.70% to $4,030.04 an ounce. Spot silver rose 0.52% to $57.96. In terms of individual stocks, memory chip stocks generally rose, with SK Hynix up about 7% and SanDisk up about 3%.

As Wall Street's earnings season begins, traders are generally cautious and the stock market is stabilizing. The market focus turned to the testimony of Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh and the latest inflation data to be released later, with investors waiting for key signals to guide the direction.

The United States' resumption of the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz against Iranian ships pushed Brent crude oil up nearly 5% during the session, hitting $87.19 a barrel.

At the same time, Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller said that if inflationary pressures persist, the authorities may need to raise interest rates in the near future, and money markets immediately priced the probability of an interest rate hike in July to about 50%.

Hebe Chen, senior market analyst at Vantage Global Prime, said: "The surge in oil prices and yields has clearly sent a signal that the inflation outlook has deteriorated.

This uncertainty explains the current swing sentiment in the market - investors are increasingly wary of the deteriorating background, but have not yet fully priced in the worst-case scenario." The surge in oil prices caused a huge shock in the market. Is the Fed closer to raising interest rates?

Oil prices soared 9% on Monday, driving the dollar to strengthen and global stock markets and precious metals to fall. It's a familiar scene, with oil prices once again becoming the driving factor in the market. After Iran announced a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz last weekend, Trump said it would resume its naval blockade against Iran.

Not only that, Trump also announced that he would impose a 20% fee on all goods transported through this (but this move lacks legal basis). Under the threat of dual blockades and toll collection by the United States and Iran, the number of ships passing through the strait has once again dropped to single digits.

The number had climbed to about 50 ships after the United States and Iran signed a memorandum of explanation in late June.

However, as the two sides continue to exchange fire, the memorandum has become "in name only." The latest data shows that oil inventories in members of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) fell to the lowest level since 1990 in May, and the U.S. strategic crude oil reserves fell to a new low in more than 40 years.

The IEA said that global observable crude oil inventories decreased by 360 million barrels from March to May. Crude oil market buffers are disappearing faster than expected, meaning the ability to absorb supply shocks in the future will inevitably decline.

Therefore, if the situation between the United States and Iran continues to escalate, and if production capacity and exports cannot be significantly improved, oil prices may face greater upward pressure than in March and April, and market volatility may intensify. U.S.

June CPI data will be released tonight (July 14) and is expected to fall from 4.2% to 3.8%, but the data cannot reflect the latest market concerns.

Surprisingly, Fed Governor Waller said this week that "if the CPI continues to be well above the 2% target, the Fed may need to raise interest rates in the near future." Morgan Stanley: S&P 500 second-quarter earnings may increase by 19%, AI drives market spread Morgan Stanley said in its latest research report that the current round of gains in U.S.

stocks is still driven by profits. As the profits of ordinary companies improve, the market's leading range of gains is expected to spread from large technology stocks to more industries.

The bank predicts that S&P 500 second-quarter earnings per share will increase by 19% year-on-year, revenue will increase by 9%, and performance is expected to exceed expectations by mid-to-high single digits. The second quarter profit forecast was raised by 2% before the financial reporting season.

This is different from the usual seasonal pattern of downward revisions, which means that companies face a higher threshold.

The profit growth rate in 2026 is expected to have risen to 24%, with revenue growth and profit margin expansion roughly equal in contribution; the "Big Seven" earnings are expected to grow by 30%, and the remaining 493 companies are expected to grow by 16%, but the latter forecast has shown signs of upward revision.

The current earnings revision breadth is positive 24%, which is at a historical high, with consumer discretionary and transportation industries performing outstandingly. Artificial intelligence remains the most important investment thread.

In the second quarter, 40% of AI adoption companies identified by Morgan Stanley disclosed at least one quantifiable benefit, up from 37% in the first quarter and 21% in the same period last year. About 25% of companies in the S&P 500 reported measurable AI benefits, up from 14% a year ago.

Relevant investments continue to cover semiconductors, infrastructure software, networks and network security. Data center storage prices are expected to rise by another 30% in the third quarter, and the visibility of cloud computing demand extends to 2028.

At the industry level, industrial capital expenditures are expanding from data centers to automation, equipment and manufacturing. Bank loans increased by about 7.5% year-on-year. In the second quarter, the scale of mergers and acquisitions, stock and bond financing increased by 64%, 107% and 19% respectively.

However, rising deposit costs may lower profit expectations. Consumption performance was better than expected, durable goods stabilized, hotel revenue per available room increased by about 5%, and tourism, gaming and aviation showed an early-cycle recovery. UBS previews financial reports of major U.S.

banks: Capital market income is strong, costs and net interest margins are key UBS expects that the second-quarter performance of large U.S.

banks will benefit overall from the recovery of investment banking and trading businesses, but long positions in the market are relatively concentrated, and fees, net interest income and management guidance will determine the stock price reaction.

JPMorgan Chase's second-quarter EPS is expected to be US$5.54, with net interest income of US$25.7 billion, investment banking fees of US$2.89 billion, a year-on-year increase of approximately 15%, and market business income of US$10.3 billion, a year-on-year increase of approximately 15%.

The company's full-year net interest income guidance is $103 billion and expense guidance is $106 billion. There is still room for revenue growth, but increased costs and the departure of executive Marianne Lake may weaken the valuation premium, and the option implied volatility is about 3.08%.

Bank of America's EPS is expected to be US$1.12 to US$1.13, with net interest income of US$16.2 billion, investment banking fees of approximately US$1.88 billion, a year-on-year increase of 30%, and market business income of approximately US$6.3 billion.

Expenses are expected to be US$18.4 billion to US$18.5 billion, which is the biggest risk; the full-year net interest income growth rate is expected to be at the upper end of the 6% to 8% guidance, and the market is concerned about whether it can maintain an operating leverage of more than 200 basis points.

Wells Fargo's EPS is expected to be US$1.73, net interest income is US$12.27 billion, and net interest margin is 2.44%, narrowing by 3 basis points month-on-month. Net interest income guidance for 2026 is approximately US$50 billion and expenses are approximately US$55.7 billion.

The stock's expectation threshold is relatively low, but if the net interest margin declines more than expected, the market may question the full-year guidance. Goldman Sachs expects EPS to be US$13.84, lower than market expectations of US$14.40; revenue is expected to be US$16.2 billion.

Market business revenue is expected to be US$9.04 billion, a year-on-year increase of 16%, of which stock business growth is approximately 25%; investment banking business revenue is US$2.75 billion, an increase of 25%. With longs crowded, fee-to-compensation ratios become key.

Citigroup's EPS is expected to be US$2.55, with net interest income of US$15.9 billion, market business income of US$6.55 billion, a year-on-year increase of approximately 11%, and investment banking expenses increasing by 17% to US$1.24 billion.

Expenses are expected to be US$14.4 billion, higher than market expectations; return on tangible common equity is expected to be 10.6%, and management's full-year target is still 10% to 11%.

Google enters NVIDIA's "turf": TPU expansion strategy targets new cloud service providers Google is pushing its self-developed AI chip TPU from internal infrastructure to the external market, and for the first time is targeting emerging cloud service providers (Neocloud) that have long relied on Nvidia GPUs on a large scale, trying to use a lower-cost and more flexible business model to compete for Nvidia's share in the AI computing power market.

According to a report by The Information on July 13, Google has recently been actively promoting TPU to a number of new cloud service providers, not only emphasizing its advantages in cost, stability, etc., but also proposing to provide financing support for data center projects and promising to lease back some TPU to reduce the financial pressure on customers to build computing infrastructure.

Faced with Google’s offensive, Nvidia quickly launched a defense. On the one hand, it discusses new financial incentives for some customers, and on the other hand, it launches a GPU leaseback guarantee plan, hoping to further stabilize the new cloud service provider ecosystem.

This competition around AI chips and cloud infrastructure is extending from product performance to financing, leasing and ecosystem. Promoting external sales of TPU does not just mean increasing chip sales.

According to reports, some management within Google believe that TPU is expected to be equivalent to about 10% of Nvidia's AI server chip business in the future, and some large AI customers, including Meta, have also been focusing on lower-priced alternatives.

A larger customer base can also increase TPU production scale and improve overall manufacturing economics. It fell more than 20% before the market opened! IBM's second-quarter revenue falls short of expectations, customers cut spending dragging down software business Preliminary data on IBM's second-quarter results disappointed the market.

U.S. Stock Futures Mixed as Bank Earnings Season Kicks Off With Strong Results

U.S. equity futures were mixed early Tuesday as investors digested a wave of better-than-expected bank earnings while monitoring rising oil prices and upcoming inflation data.

S&P 500 futures ($ES) were nearly flat, while Nasdaq 100 futures ($NQ) edged higher. Dow Jones Industrial Average futures ($YM) slipped modestly.

Bank Earnings Provide Support

Major banks delivered solid second-quarter results, helping offset some caution in the broader market. Goldman Sachs Group Inc. ($GS) reported record profit of $6.63 billion, up 78 percent from a year earlier, with revenue rising 39 percent to $20.34 billion. The bank benefited significantly from equity underwriting fees, which more than doubled thanks in part to the SpaceX IPO.

JPMorgan Chase & Co. ($JPM) posted a 41 percent jump in profit, while Bank of America Corp. ($BAC) saw profit rise 27 percent, helped by record sales and trading revenue. Wells Fargo & Co. ($WFC) reported a 17 percent increase in profit and record investment banking fees.

The results suggest Wall Street operations remain a bright spot for the big banks, even as broader economic uncertainty persists.

Oil Continues to Climb

Oil prices extended gains amid ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. West Texas Intermediate crude ($CL) rose more than 2 percent, while Brent crude ($BRN) climbed over 3 percent. Traders are watching for any further impact on energy markets from developments in the Strait of Hormuz region.

Key Data and Events Today

Markets are awaiting the June consumer price index report at 8:30 a.m. Eastern, which could influence views on inflation and Federal Reserve policy. Fed Chair Kevin Warsh is also scheduled to testify before Congress.

Other Fed speakers, including Governor Michael Barr and Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, are set to appear later in the day.

What to Watch

Investors will focus on how the market reacts to the CPI print and Warsh’s comments, as well as any follow-through from the bank earnings. Rising oil prices add another layer of complexity ahead of inflation data, while the strength in bank results could provide some support for equities if the economic backdrop remains stable.

Brent just surged nearly 13% in two sessions to $86. The Iran ceasefire that markets spent three weeks pricing in has effectively broken down.
US strikes on Iranian military sites tied to Hormuz attacks, combined with a reinstated blockade on Iranian vessels, have repriced energy risk overnight. Exxon climbed 4.1% on the news. Nvidia fell 3.5% as tech absorbed the broader risk-off move.
Here’s the collision that matters today specifically: CPI prints this morning, Federal Reserve Chair Warsh testifies before Congress, and JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Goldman Sachs all report earnings — all on the same day that oil just moved 13% in 48 hours.
Each of those four events would move markets independently. Together they create a stress test for whether the current rally in risk assets was pricing a world where energy inflation was fading. It wasn’t fading. It paused.
The CLARITY Act revised version is also expected in Congress next week — crypto regulation’s most important legislative moment in years arriving into the same week as an energy shock and bank earnings.
Markets rarely give you this many binary events simultaneously. The question isn’t which one matters most. It’s whether portfolios built for one scenario can absorb all four at once.

## US Stock Market Summary: Monday, July 13
The three major US stock indices fell across the board, led by tech stocks—particularly the semiconductor sector—as markets were dragged down by inflation concerns triggered by escalating US-Iran geopolitical tensions and surging oil prices. The Nasdaq Composite suffered the sharpest decline, snapping its previous three-day winning streak.
### Closing Performance of Major Indices
 * **Dow Jones Industrial Average**: Closed at **52,498.64**, down 138.37 points (-0.26%).
 * **S&P 500 Index**: Closed at **7,515.34**, down approximately 0.79%.
 * **Nasdaq Composite Index**: Closed at **25,873.18**, down approximately 1.55%.
### Key Market Drivers
 * **Escalating US-Iran Geopolitical Tensions**
   Over the weekend, both sides exchanged airstrikes. The US took new actions near the Strait of Hormuz, while Iran retaliated against US allies. Donald Trump announced that "the ceasefire is over" and reinstated a shipping blockade on Iranian vessels through the Strait of Hormuz (including a 20% transit fee), sparking severe market anxiety over the security of critical energy corridors.
 * **Surging Oil Prices**
   Brent crude briefly spiked above $83 per barrel, and WTI crude rose past $78, marking significant single-day gains that approached double digits at certain points. The jump in oil prices fueled inflation expectations, prompting the market to recalibrate its outlook on the probability of Federal Reserve rate cuts.
 * **Collective Pullback in Semiconductor and Tech Stocks**
   South Korean chip giant SK Hynix plummeted around 9% to 15% on its first day of trading following its US listing, marking a record single-day drop and dragging down the entire US chip sector. Major semiconductor stocks like Intel, Micron, AMD, and ARM generally slid by over 4% to 6%, putting the heaviest pressure on the Nasdaq.

 * **Other Sectors**: Supported by rising oil prices, the energy sector showed relative resilience. Conversely, several high-growth tech stocks (e.g., Shopify, AppLovin) fell in tandem with the broader market.

> The US stock market exhibited a clear **"risk-off"** tone on July 13, with geopolitics and energy prices taking center stage, prompting profit-taking across the tech and AI sectors. While the overall losses remain contained, short-term market volatility is likely to intensify.

美股7月13日（周一）总结

美股三大指数全线下挫，科技股尤其是半导体板块领跌，受美伊地缘政治紧张和油价暴涨引发的通胀担忧拖累。纳斯达克指数跌幅最大，结束了此前三连涨。

主要指数收盘表现
道琼斯工业平均指数 (Dow Jones)：收于 **52,498.64 点，下跌 138.37 点（-0.26%）。
标普500指数 (S&P 500)：收于 **7,515.34 点，下跌约 0.79%。
纳斯达克综合指数 (Nasdaq Composite)：收于 **25,873.18 点，下跌约 1.55%。

主要驱动因素
美伊地缘政治紧张升级  
   周末美伊双方互有空袭，美国在霍尔木兹海峡附近采取新行动，伊朗则对美盟友进行报复。特朗普宣布“停火已结束”，并重启对伊朗通过霍尔木兹海峡的航运封锁（含20%过境费），引发市场对能源通道安全的担忧。

油价大幅上涨  
   布伦特原油一度飙升至83美元/桶以上，WTI原油升至78美元以上，单日涨幅显著（部分时段接近两位数）。油价上涨加剧通胀预期，市场对美联储降息的概率出现调整。

半导体与科技股集体回调  
   韩国芯片巨头 SK Hynix 在首日美股上市后暴跌约9-15%（创纪录最大单日跌幅），拖累美股芯片板块。英特尔（Intel）、美光（Micron）、AMD、ARM等芯片股普遍下跌4%-6%以上。纳斯达克因此承压最大。

其他市场亮点
银行股**：银行财报季即将开启，多家大行（如摩根大通、高盛等）出现回调。
其他板块**：能源板块因油价上涨相对抗跌；部分科技成长股（如Shopify、AppLovin）也随大盘走弱。
宏观背景**：市场正等待周二（7月14日）的6月CPI通胀数据，以及即将公布的银行财报。标普500二季度盈利预期仍较高（同比增约20.9%），但油价和地缘风险带来短期不确定性。

总体评价：7月13日美股呈现“风险厌恶”行情，地缘政治与能源价格成为主导因素，科技/AI板块出现获利了结。整体跌幅尚属可控，但短期波动性可能加大。

**Stagflation Risk Through the November 2026 Midterms**

The U.S. is already in a mild stagflationary regime, and the risk is rising through the midterms. The Iran conflict remains the dominant variable.

**Current Conditions:**
- Headline CPI hit +4.2% YoY in May, driven heavily by energy costs tied to the Hormuz disruption.
- Growth is slowing but not collapsing. Jobs growth has decelerated while consumer sentiment sits near record lows.
- Real wages are being eroded, especially for lower-income households, by elevated food and energy prices.
- The Fed, now under Chair Warsh, has shifted to a clear **higher-for-longer** stance, with markets pricing in a higher chance of a late-2026 hike.

**Key Stagflation Drivers to Watch:**
- Sustained or worsening Hormuz/shipping disruptions
- Sticky food inflation and tariff pass-through effects
- Further cooling in the labor market
- Fed policy remaining restrictive for longer than expected

**Scenario Probabilities Through November 2026:**

- **Base Case (Tense Stalemate) — ~45–50% probability**: Oil stays in the $75–$90 range. Inflation remains sticky between 3.5–4.2%. Growth slows further to around 1–1.5% GDP, with unemployment rising gradually. The Fed stays hawkish and holds rates higher for longer, with a possible late-2026 hike still on the table. Gold, Silver, Oil, and the Dow are the most likely relative winners in this scenario.

- **Escalation — ~35–40% probability**: Oil spikes toward $95–$120+. Inflation moves above 4.5% as energy costs pass through more aggressively. Growth stalls or contracts, with faster job losses. The Fed tilts even more hawkish. Gold and Silver would likely see strong upside as safe-haven assets, while Oil would benefit significantly from any sustained Hormuz disruption.

- **De-escalation — ~10–15% probability**: Oil falls back toward $65–$78. Inflation trends lower toward 3%. Growth stabilizes or rebounds modestly. The Fed gains more room to cut rates later in 2026. Bitcoin, Nasdaq, and the broader S&P 500 would likely see the strongest relief rally in this scenario.

**Asset Implications:**

- **Gold & Silver**: Strong structural support. Central bank buying combined with long-term petrodollar erosion (Gulf infrastructure damage + repeated leadership strikes) provides a powerful floor. Silver has the highest beta and often leads on geopolitical spikes.

- **Bitcoin**: Mixed in the short term as a risk asset due to higher real yields and risk-off flows, but structurally supported by the “digital gold + de-dollarization” narrative. MVRV-Z remains deeply undervalued, suggesting any sharp flush would likely be a high-conviction accumulation zone.

- **Equities**: Broad pressure with clear sector divergence. Energy and defense names should hold up best. Growth and tech (especially Nasdaq) face the most downside risk from higher real yields and risk-off rotation. The Dow remains the most resilient major index due to its sector mix.

**Bottom Line:**
Stagflation risk through the midterms is material and rising. A prolonged tense stalemate or further escalation would keep inflation sticky while slowing growth and pressuring the labor market.

In this environment, **Gold and Silver** stand out as the clearest structural winners due to their dual role as inflation hedges and beneficiaries of petrodollar erosion. Bitcoin should hold up better than growth equities over the full period thanks to undervaluation and its long-term narrative. Nasdaq faces the highest downside risk in any sustained risk-off move.

The situation remains highly fluid. Any meaningful de-escalation in the Strait of Hormuz would materially reduce these risks.

The oil market has not been competitive for about 155 years. It has been about the control of production to influence oil prices:  the market power of oil producers along with government intervention has always influenced the market.  The brief times when the market was truly competitive were marred by chaos, extreme volatility, high costs, and a decline in efficiency. 

In recent years, however, China, the world’s largest crude oil importer, started changing the game.  

Using its inventories, including the SPR, China single-handedly transformed the oil market structure from oligopoly to oligopsony. In the first, producers have market power, while in the second type of market, buyers have market power.  If the power of both sides is equal, the price will be similar to the price in the market as if it were competitive with thousands of sellers and buyers. In the case of China, it can prevent prices from increasing as it did in 2017, 2018, and 2021. And we believe that Beijing will also adopt a similar behavior in 2023.

The use of inventories makes more oil available to Chinese refiners and others, as it also reduces oil imports, and oil prices, while generating significant profits in the process. If China plays on demand seasonality, it can also prevent oil prices from rising further and lower them instead.  Once prices are low, China will then refill the SPR.

Under such a market structure where OPEC+ is an oligopoly and China is an oligopsony, OPEC sets the price floor, while China determines the price ceiling.

At present, China is awash with cheap oil from Russia and Iran and has about 891 million barrels (mb) of crude oil above the ground, according to Kpler’s data.  While the amount of oil in caverns below the ground remains unknown, it is fair to say that China’s caverns contain up to 130 mb.

The charts below tell the story of the role of China’s crude oil inventories in the oil market.
